suggestions.description: Add a pre-filter to exempt non-answer comments (replies, thank-yous, meta-comments) from the answer-quality checklist entirely. All labeled compliant examples are short follow-up comments, not actual answers, and the current deterministic regex would incorrectly flag them as too short.

proposed_change.description: Is the answer too short or superficial to be considered comprehensive and informative?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: A good answer will go further than a simple short sentence.
proposed_change.note: This parent node should only be evaluated against top-level answers (direct replies to the original post), not follow-up comments, thank-you replies, or meta-discussion. Downstream subjective checks should apply the same scoping.

suggestions.reasoning: Every single labeled compliant example in the dataset is a short follow-up comment, thank-you note, or meta-discussion reply (e.g., 'Thank you so much for your response!', 'Well said. Thanks', 'Thanks! I'll check those out.'). None are actual answers to historical questions. The current regex `^.{0,300}$` would match all of them and push them into the subjective violation checks. A scoping note clarifying that the entire branch applies only to actual answers (top-level replies to the OP) is essential to prevent mass over-triggering on compliant follow-up comments.

suggestions.description: Add a new deterministic pre-filter child that exempts comments which are clearly follow-up replies (e.g., thank-yous, acknowledgements, meta-questions) from further quality checks. These should be classified as compliant automatically and skip the subjective children.

proposed_change.description: Is this comment a non-answer follow-up (e.g., a thank-you, acknowledgement, or meta-comment) rather than a substantive historical answer?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: Answers in this subreddit are expected to be of a level that historians would provide

suggestions.reasoning: All 9 labeled compliant examples match patterns of follow-up or acknowledgement comments: 'Wow, thank you so much', 'Thank you! That's fascinating.', 'Thanks! I'll check those out.', 'Well said. Don't know why i struggled...thanks', 'Although I am familiar with...Thank you for pointing it out.', 'I actually read that book...', 'This might not be quite what you are looking for...', etc. A deterministic early-exit rule that identifies these non-answer patterns and marks them as passing would prevent false positives across all three downstream subjective checks.

suggestions.description: Update the subjective prompt to explicitly instruct the model to return a low violation score (not violating) for comments that are clearly follow-up replies, acknowledgements, or meta-discussion rather than actual answers to historical questions.

proposed_change.description: Does the short answer fail to provide meaningful historical explanation or context?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: good answers aren't good just because they are right – they are good because they explain

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment is (a) an actual answer to a historical question and (b) if so, whether it is too brief or superficial to meet the standards of a comprehensive, historian-level response. IMPORTANT: If the comment is a follow-up reply, thank-you note, acknowledgement, meta-question, or casual banter rather than a substantive answer to the original historical question, score it as 0 (not violating). Only score substantive answer attempts. Does the answer merely state a fact without explaining context, causation, significance, or relevant historiographical background?
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The comment is clearly intended as an answer to the historical question AND is only one or two sentences that state a conclusion without elaboration; (2) The answer provides no historical context or explanation of why or how; (3) The answer could be dismissed as a 'yes/no' or trivially short reply; (4) The answer does not go beyond what a casual Wikipedia skim would provide. Score 0 (not violating) when: the comment is a thank-you, acknowledgement, follow-up question, meta-discussion, or casual reply that is not attempting to answer the historical question.

suggestions.reasoning: The compliant examples ('Wow, thank you so much', 'Thank you! That's fascinating.', 'Well said. thanks', etc.) are all follow-up comments, not answers. Without explicit instruction to exempt non-answers, the LLM evaluator may still score them as violating because they are short and lack historical explanation. Adding explicit guidance prevents over-triggering on these compliant follow-up comments.

suggestions.description: Update the subjective prompt for the explanation-quality check to explicitly exempt non-answer comments (follow-ups, thank-yous, meta-discussion) from violation scoring.

proposed_change.description: Does the answer fail to explain or elaborate, merely stating facts without context or reasoning?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: you should seek not just to be right, but to explain

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment is a substantive attempt to answer a historical question. IMPORTANT: If the comment is a follow-up reply, thank-you, acknowledgement, meta-comment, or casual banter rather than an answer to the original historical question, score it as 0 (not violating) — these are not subject to answer-quality standards. For comments that ARE substantive answer attempts: evaluate whether the answer provides genuine historical explanation and context, or whether it merely states a conclusion. Does the answer explain the 'why', 'how', or broader historical significance of its claims, as a trained historian would?
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The comment is a genuine answer attempt AND asserts a historical claim without explaining underlying causes, mechanisms, or context; (2) The answer reads like a list of facts with no analytical thread; (3) The answer does not engage with historiographical nuance or scholarly perspective; (4) The answer could be considered a bare assertion rather than a historical explanation. Score 0 (not violating) when: the comment is a thank-you, acknowledgement, follow-up question, meta-discussion, or casual reply not attempting to answer the historical question. Score lower also when the answer contextualizes events, discusses sources, explains causation, or situates the topic within broader historical developments.

suggestions.reasoning: Same pattern as the previous check — all compliant examples are follow-up comments, not answers. The explanation-quality check would otherwise incorrectly flag them as lacking historical explanation. Explicit exemption logic is needed.

suggestions.description: Update the citation check's subjective prompt to explicitly exempt non-answer comments from the citation requirement, since follow-up replies and thank-you comments are not expected to cite sources.

proposed_change.description: Does the answer lack any citation, source reference, or acknowledgment of scholarly basis?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: You should cite or quote sources where possible.

logic.prompt_template: First, determine whether this comment is a substantive answer to a historical question. IMPORTANT: If the comment is a follow-up reply, thank-you, acknowledgement, meta-comment, or casual banter, score it as 0 (not violating) — citation standards apply only to substantive answer attempts. For comments that ARE substantive answer attempts: evaluate whether the answer makes any attempt to cite, reference, or acknowledge sources for its historical claims. Does the answer include book titles, author names, academic works, primary sources, or any indication of the scholarly basis for its assertions?
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The comment is a genuine answer attempt AND makes multiple historical claims with zero sourcing or attribution; (2) There is no mention of any historian, book, article, or primary source; (3) The answer reads as purely from personal memory with no scholarly grounding. Score 0 (not violating) when: the comment is a thank-you, acknowledgement, follow-up question, meta-discussion, or casual reply not attempting to answer the historical question. Score lower when the answer cites at least one work, names a historian, quotes a source, or otherwise signals engagement with historical scholarship. Note: informal but substantive answers with some sourcing gesture are acceptable.

suggestions.reasoning: Follow-up comments like 'Thank you! That's fascinating.' or 'Well said. thanks' would trivially fail a citation check since they contain no sources. However, they are compliant by label. The citation check must be scoped to actual answer attempts only, not casual replies.

suggestions.description: Add a pre-check to identify replies/comments that are not substantive answers (e.g., thank-you messages, acknowledgements, brief conversational follow-ups) and exempt them from the in-depth answer rule entirely. All labeled compliant examples are short social comments, not historical answers, yet the current deterministic check (<=300 chars) would flag most of them.
suggestions.reasoning: Every single compliant example is a short reply comment — 'Thank you!', 'Well said.', 'Thanks! I'll check those out.', 'I'll see what I can do about keeping the questions coming!', etc. These are social/conversational comments, not substantive historical answers. The current deterministic regex (^.{0,300}$) would match all of them and potentially route them into the subjective violation checks. The checklist needs a way to distinguish 'short because it is a social reply' from 'short because it is a poor answer attempt'.

proposed_change.description: Is the answer too short or superficial to provide meaningful context and explanation?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: An in-depth answer is usually more than a sentence or two.

suggestions.description: Add a new first child under the short-answer parent that detects social/conversational replies (thank-yous, acknowledgements, brief follow-up chatter) and immediately marks them compliant, preventing them from being evaluated as insufficient historical answers.
suggestions.reasoning: All 9 labeled compliant examples are short social comments replying to other users — expressions of thanks, acknowledgement, brief conversational remarks — none of which are attempts at historical answers. Without this exemption, the subjective child checks (acfdac70 and 2e9c67f7) risk incorrectly flagging these as violations. Examples include: 'Wow, thank you so much for your response!', 'Thank you! That's fascinating.', 'Thanks! I'll check those out.', 'Well said. Don't know why I struggled so hard to come up with the terminology. thanks', 'Although I am familiar with the word tawhid as well, I never thought to make the connection. Thank you for pointing it out.'

proposed_change.description: Is this comment a social/conversational reply (e.g., a thank-you, acknowledgement, or brief follow-up) rather than an attempt at a substantive historical answer?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: An in-depth answer gives context to the events being discussed so that someone who is unfamiliar with the area can understand.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment is a social or conversational reply rather than an attempt to provide a substantive historical answer. Examples of social replies include: expressing thanks or appreciation to another commenter, brief acknowledgements ('Well said', 'Good point'), short follow-up remarks that do not attempt to answer the original question, or comments that simply engage socially without contributing historical content.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to be a social reply, i.e., COMPLIANT and should be exempted from the in-depth answer rule) when: (1) The comment expresses gratitude, thanks, or appreciation; (2) The comment is a brief acknowledgement of something said by another user; (3) The comment does not attempt to answer the original historical question at all; (4) The comment is clearly addressed as a reply to another commenter rather than as an answer to the OP. Score lower (less likely to be a social reply) when the comment, however short, is making a factual or analytical claim about a historical topic in response to the original question.

suggestions.description: Update the order of this item to 1 (after the new social-reply exemption check at order 0) so it only evaluates short posts that have already been confirmed as genuine answer attempts, not social comments.
suggestions.reasoning: With the new social-reply exemption child added at order 0, this subjective check for short/superficial answers should run second (order 1) to avoid evaluating comments that have already been identified as harmless social replies. This prevents false positives on the many compliant thank-you/acknowledgement comments seen in the labeled examples.

suggestions.description: Refine the rubric for the link-only check to explicitly exempt cases where the commenter acknowledges they are not a subject-matter expert, defers to others, and signals the limitation of their contribution — as seen in compliant example df0xrnz (CaptainPyjamaShark), who provides a link but openly admits it may break protocol and invites an expert to respond.
suggestions.reasoning: Compliant example df0xrnz posts a link to another subreddit's answer and a brief summary ('you're right to treat it with a grain of salt') without deep original analysis, which could trigger this check. However, the comment demonstrates self-awareness about its limitations, defers to an expert, and is transparent about its shortcomings. The current rubric does not account for such self-aware, deferential link posts. Additionally, compliant example 604f6958 posts a link and an ISBN with a caveat about uncertainty of the source — this borderline case should also score lower given the explicit sourcing attempt.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this answer contributes original in-depth explanation, or whether it mainly just points to an external source (e.g. a link, a quoted passage, or a paraphrased article summary) without the author providing their own substantive historical analysis and context.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The answer is primarily or entirely a link to an external article or resource with no substantive commentary; (2) The answer is mainly a block quotation with little or no original commentary; (3) The answer reads as a thin paraphrase or summary of a single Google-found article with no independent expertise demonstrated; (4) The author does not demonstrate personal knowledge of the topic beyond basic web search skills. Score lower (less likely to violate) when: (1) Links or quotations are used to supplement a substantial original explanation that demonstrates expertise; (2) The author explicitly acknowledges the limitations of their knowledge, defers to a named expert, and is transparent that their contribution is supplementary rather than authoritative; (3) The author provides a specific bibliographic citation (e.g., an ISBN or book title) alongside their summary, suggesting genuine engagement with source material rather than a casual Google search.

suggestions.description: Add a pre-filter to exclude comments that are clearly not answers (e.g., thank-you replies, conversational follow-ups, joke responses) before running placeholder checks. All labeled examples are compliant short conversational comments, not answers at all — the checklist needs to handle non-answer comments gracefully.
suggestions.reasoning: All 7 labeled examples are compliant and consist of short conversational replies (e.g., 'Thanks!', 'Well said.', 'Wow, thank you so much'), joke responses, or follow-up comments — none are answers to historical questions. The current checklist has no mechanism to short-circuit evaluation for non-answer comments, risking over-triggering on phrases like 'I'll check those out' (example c058e114) matching 'I'll add/edit' patterns, or 'I'll see what I can do' (example b05d8a46) matching placeholder signals. A pre-filter deterministic step should detect when a comment is clearly not an answer post and exit early as compliant.

proposed_change.description: Is the comment structured as an answer to the thread question, rather than a conversational reply, acknowledgment, or meta-comment?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: An answer should be full and complete in and of itself.

suggestions.description: Add a new first-order subjective child check that screens out non-answer comments (thank-yous, conversational replies, jokes, meta-comments) before evaluating placeholder status. This prevents the downstream placeholder checks from being applied to comments that are not answers in the first place.
suggestions.reasoning: Every single labeled compliant example is a non-answer comment: simple thank-you messages (dfgvu4s, dfjp6tm, dfkxbx8), a conversational joke (dfdt2im), a light conversational reply (b05d8a46), a partial attempt that self-acknowledges it may not be appropriate (df0xrnz), and a short note about a book (92b58c39 — somewhat closer to an answer but still brief and conversational). None of these should reach the placeholder evaluation steps. A gating subjective check asking 'Is this comment an actual attempt to answer the thread question?' would prevent false positives on conversational content.

proposed_change.description: Is this comment actually an attempt to answer the historical question posed in the thread, rather than a thank-you, conversational reply, joke, or meta-comment about the thread?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: An answer should be full and complete in and of itself.

logic.prompt_template: Determine whether this comment is an actual attempt to answer the historical question in the thread, or whether it is instead a conversational reply (e.g., thank-you, acknowledgment, joke, meta-comment, question redirect, or off-topic remark). Only flag it for further review if it appears to be an answer attempt.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to be an answer attempt requiring further review) when: (1) The comment directly addresses the historical question with factual content; (2) The comment provides analysis, sources, or historical context in response to the question; (3) The comment is clearly posted as a response to the question, not to another comment's conversational content. Score lower (not an answer — stop review) when: (1) The comment is a simple thank-you or acknowledgment; (2) The comment is a joke or light-hearted aside; (3) The comment is a meta-remark about the subreddit rules or redirects to another user; (4) The comment is a brief conversational follow-up not containing substantive historical content.

suggestions.description: Update the order of the explicit-placeholder subjective check to run after the new non-answer gating check, and refine the rubric to more explicitly exclude conversational phrases like 'I'll check those out' or 'I'll see what I can do' that could superficially match placeholder patterns.
suggestions.reasoning: Examples like dfjp6tm ('Thanks! I'll check those out.') and b05d8a46 ('I'll see what I can do about keeping the questions coming!') contain phrases that could superficially match placeholder regex patterns ('I'll' + verb) but are clearly not placeholder answers — they are conversational replies. The rubric should be strengthened to explicitly note that first-person future-tense phrases in conversational (non-answer) context do not constitute placeholder violations.

proposed_change.description: Does the answer signal it is intentionally partial or will be expanded later, confirming a placeholder violation?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: Do not provide a part-answer as a "placeholder" to come back to expand on later.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment is a partial or placeholder answer — one that is intentionally incomplete, explicitly promises further elaboration, or is posted mainly to 'reserve a spot' in the thread rather than to provide a full, standalone answer.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The comment explicitly states the author will return to add more content to their answer; (2) The comment describes itself as a partial, quick, or preliminary answer to the question; (3) The comment asks others to wait or check back for the full answer; (4) The comment is substantively thin and signals awareness that it is incomplete as an answer. Score lower when: (1) Matched keywords appear in context that is clearly not a placeholder (e.g., 'I will add a citation' in an otherwise comprehensive answer); (2) The future-tense phrase appears in a conversational reply, joke, or thank-you comment rather than an answer; (3) The comment is a non-answer conversational exchange (e.g., 'I'll check those out', 'I'll see what I can do about questions') — these are not placeholder answers.

suggestions.description: Update the subjective completeness check to explicitly exclude non-answer comments from being scored as incomplete answers, and clarify that short conversational replies are not 'partial answers' under this rule.
suggestions.reasoning: Example 92b58c39 (Qeeknique's comment about Lili Marleen) is labeled compliant but is a short, somewhat uncertain answer that self-acknowledges its limitations ('This might not be quite what you are looking for', 'Im not sure there was too much about this specific song in this very book'). This could score borderline on the completeness check. Similarly, df0xrnz acknowledges it may be 'breaking AskHistorians protocol'. The rubric should be updated to clarify that comments which acknowledge uncertainty or limitations but still provide substantive content are not automatically violations, and that non-answer conversational replies are entirely outside scope.

proposed_change.description: Is the answer substantively incomplete — lacking depth, context, or full explanation — even without an explicit placeholder signal?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: If you can not answer the question fully, wait for someone who can.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment constitutes a full, standalone answer to the question posed in the thread, or whether it is a partial answer that leaves the question meaningfully unanswered. Consider whether the response could stand on its own without further expansion. Note: if the comment is not an answer attempt at all (e.g., a thank-you, joke, or conversational reply), it should not be evaluated here.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The answer addresses only a narrow slice of the question without acknowledging other important aspects; (2) The answer is very short (1–3 sentences) for a complex historical question with no explanation of why brevity is appropriate; (3) The answer raises points or evidence but does not develop them into a coherent response; (4) The comment reads more like a prompt for discussion or a hint than an actual answer. Score lower when: (1) The question is simple and a short answer is genuinely complete; (2) The answer is thorough even if it notes it cannot cover every sub-topic; (3) The comment acknowledges uncertainty about a source or scope limitation but still provides substantive historical content; (4) The comment is a conversational reply, acknowledgment, or non-answer comment — these are out of scope for this check entirely.

suggestions.description: Update the rubric and prompt to explicitly exclude short social/conversational replies (thank-you notes, acknowledgments, brief follow-ups) from triggering as joke/humor violations, since all labeled examples are compliant short social comments rather than jokes.
suggestions.reasoning: All five labeled examples are short, non-humorous social replies ('Thank you!', 'Well said.', 'Wow, thank you so much', etc.). One example (dd8147f3) contains a light sarcastic remark marked with '/s', yet is labeled compliant. The current checklist item's rubric could over-trigger on these posts because they lack 'substantive historical content', even though they are not jokes or humorous remarks. The rubric needs to clarify that brief conversational/social comments (acknowledgments, thank-yous, follow-ups) are a distinct category from jokes/humor and should not be flagged under this rule. The rule targets humor specifically, not the absence of historical substance in general.

proposed_change.description: Is the post solely a joke, humorous remark, or flippant comment without substantive historical content?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: A post should not consist only of a joke, a humorous remark, or a flippant comment.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this post consists solely of a joke, humorous remark, or flippant comment, with no substantive historical content. Does the post make any genuine attempt to answer a historical question, provide historical context, or contribute meaningful information beyond humor? Note: short social or conversational replies (e.g. expressions of thanks, acknowledgments, brief follow-ups to a prior exchange) are NOT jokes or humorous remarks and should NOT be flagged under this rule, even if they lack historical substance. This rule targets posts whose PRIMARY purpose is to amuse or be witty.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The post is entirely or almost entirely a punchline, quip, or comedic remark clearly intended to amuse; (2) The post contains no factual historical information, analysis, or context AND its evident purpose is humor rather than social acknowledgment; (3) The post's evident purpose is to amuse rather than to inform or to engage socially; (4) Any historical references present are incidental to the joke rather than substantive. Score lower (less likely to violate) when: (1) Humor is used as a framing device alongside genuine historical content; (2) The post includes a thorough, sourced, or well-contextualized historical answer even if it has a witty tone; (3) The humor enhances rather than replaces historical substance; (4) The post is a brief social/conversational reply such as a thank-you, acknowledgment, or expression of appreciation — these are not jokes and do not fall under this rule; (5) A sarcastic or joking aside is clearly marked (e.g. '/s') and is secondary to an otherwise appropriate comment.

suggestions.description: Tighten the rubric to avoid over-triggering on comments that include a cited source alongside hedging language, since sourced contributions should score lower even when hedging phrases are present.
suggestions.reasoning: The compliant example (48c86141) contains classic hedging phrases ('Im not sure there was too much about this specific song in this very book but I quite sure it was mentioned there') AND presents uncertain information as a statement. However, it also provides a specific ISBN-cited source and substantive historical content (Lili Marleen), making it a genuine attempt at an answer rather than a rule violation. The current rubric does not explicitly instruct the model to score lower when a cited source is provided, risking a false-positive flag. Adding that guidance will reduce over-triggering on sourced-but-hedged answers.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment presents uncertain or unverified information as a statement rather than as a question. The rule requires that if a commenter is not sure about something and is not genuinely answering, they must frame their comment as a question — not as a hedged assertion like 'I'm not sure if this is true, but...' or 'Someone will correct me if I'm wrong, but...'
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The comment uses hedging phrases like 'I'm not sure if this is true' or 'correct me if I'm wrong' but does NOT end or reframe as a genuine question; (2) The comment appears to be offering unverified information or speculation disguised as a contribution rather than asking a follow-up question; (3) The commenter seems to want validation rather than genuinely seeking clarification; (4) The comment provides no cited source or substantive supporting detail for its claim. Score lower when: the comment is clearly phrased as a sincere question, even if prefaced with uncertainty; OR when the comment includes a specific cited source (e.g. a book title, ISBN, URL, or named reference) and makes a substantive attempt to answer the original question, even if the author hedges minor details about that source.

suggestions.description: Update the rubric to explicitly score lower when the comment provides a cited source and substantive on-topic content, preventing over-triggering on compliant sourced answers that happen to acknowledge minor uncertainty about the source details.
suggestions.reasoning: The compliant example (48c86141) could also trigger this item because it introduces related historical content as statements rather than questions and hedges about whether the specific detail appeared in the cited book. However, it clearly attempts to answer the original question with a named book and ISBN. The current rubric's 'sourced answer' guidance is present but insufficiently emphasised — adding an explicit note about partial-source uncertainty (hedging about whether a detail appeared in an otherwise cited work) will further reduce false positives.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment is a non-answer that should have been framed as a question but was not. The rule states: if the commenter is not actually answering the original question, their comment must be phrased as a question. Does this comment appear to be a follow-up thought, a related claim, or a tangential observation that is not a genuine answer, but is written as a statement rather than a question?
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The comment does not substantively answer the original question; (2) The comment introduces a related but unverified claim or anecdote as a statement with no supporting source; (3) The comment could reasonably have been posed as a question (e.g. 'Wasn't X also related to Y?') but was instead written as a declarative assertion; (4) The comment seems to be testing or sharing a half-remembered fact rather than contributing a sourced answer. Score lower when: the comment is clearly a question; OR when it is a substantive, sourced answer to the original post — including cases where the author hedges only about minor details within an otherwise cited source (e.g. 'I'm not sure this specific detail was in this book, but here is the ISBN'); OR when the comment provides relevant on-topic historical or factual content alongside a named reference even if the author expresses minor uncertainty about that reference.

suggestions.description: Update the rubric and prompt to explicitly account for short text-only comments (no media at all) so that brief compliant replies like thank-you notes are not accidentally over-triggered by the visual-media check.
suggestions.reasoning: All three labeled examples are compliant and contain no media whatsoever — they are short, text-only acknowledgment comments ('Wow, thank you so much for your response!', 'Thank you! That's fascinating.', 'Well said. Don't know why i struggled so hard to come up with the terminology. thanks'). The current checklist item is specifically scoped to image/video/gif/meme content, so it should not flag these, but the prompt_template and rubric do not explicitly call out the 'no media present' case as a clear non-violation. Adding an explicit note that comments with zero media attachments or links are outside the scope of this check reduces the risk of the LLM scorer over-triggering on short comments that happen to lack substantive written content but also lack any visual media.

proposed_change.description: Does the comment consist only or mainly of an image, video, gif, or meme without substantive written content?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: Any comment which consists only or mainly of an image or a video or a meme will be deleted.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment consists only or mainly of an image, video, gif, or meme, with little to no substantive written explanation. Does the comment rely on visual media as its primary or sole content rather than a written answer? IMPORTANT: If the comment contains NO media at all (no images, no videos, no gifs, no meme links or embeds), it does NOT violate this rule regardless of how short or non-substantive the text is. This check is exclusively about comments whose primary payload is visual media.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The comment contains only an image, gif, video, or meme link/embed and no written text; (2) The comment contains a small amount of text (e.g. a caption or one-liner) that does not stand alone as a substantive answer, with a visual being the main payload; (3) The visual content is a generic internet meme, reaction gif, or illustrative image not directly supporting a written historical answer. Score lower (less likely to violate) when: (1) The comment contains a detailed written answer and the image or video is clearly supplementary evidence supporting that answer; (2) The visual is a primary source or documentary clip explicitly cited and explained in the text; (3) The comment contains NO media whatsoever — even if the text is very short or a simple acknowledgment, the absence of any visual media means this rule does not apply.

suggestions.reasoning: All five labeled examples are marked compliant yet are very short, lack legal substance, and could superficially appear joke-like or off-topic (e.g., 'How else do you get the dog?', 'Oh username check out', 'How about a large Farva?'). The rubric should be clarified to distinguish between pure jokes/memes (violating) and short conversational remarks or reactions that are simply not legal advice (compliant). logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment contains any substantive legal information, advice, or guidance. Does the comment consist entirely of jokes, memes, puns, sarcasm, or comedic bits without offering any legal insight, relevant information, or meaningful contribution to the legal question being asked? Note: very short conversational remarks, reactions, or tangential observations — while not legal advice — are not necessarily violations unless they are clearly structured as jokes or humor intended to mock or derail the thread.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The comment is entirely or almost entirely a joke, pun, meme, or comedic aside with no legal substance whatsoever and is clearly intended to be humorous rather than helpful. (2) The comment makes fun of the situation, OP, or a party in a purely comedic way with no attempt to engage with the legal question. (3) The comment is a reference to a movie, TV show, meme, or pop culture solely for comedic effect with no legal relevance. Score lower (less likely to violate) when: (1) The comment contains a joke but also includes genuine legal information or advice. (2) The comment is a brief conversational remark or reaction that, while not legal advice, is not structured as a joke or pun. (3) The comment politely declines to advise while directing the user to seek proper counsel. (4) The comment is a short tangential observation that does not derail or mock the thread.

suggestions.description: Clarify the rule text to explicitly note that brief conversational remarks or reactions are tolerated, but deliberate jokes, puns, memes, or comedic bits with no legal substance are not.
suggestions.reasoning: The five compliant examples ('How else do you get the dog?', 'So I guess OP is fairly-significantly-fucked?', 'My takeaway from all this is that the Amish are, in fact, assholes.', 'Oh username check out', 'How about a large Farva?') are all tolerated despite lacking legal content. logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this post or comment consists primarily of negative, hostile, dismissive, or flaming content directed at the OP or other users, and lacks substantive legal advice or helpful guidance. Does it mainly serve to criticize, belittle, or shame the poster rather than offer actionable legal information or perspective? Note: short jokes, meta-comments, off-topic quips, expressions of sympathy/frustration about third parties (not the OP), or brief tangential remarks should NOT be flagged unless they are hostile toward the OP or another user and add no value whatsoever to the thread.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The post or comment is predominantly insulting, condescending, or mocking toward the OP or other community members; (2) It offers no meaningful legal advice, information, or direction; (3) It expresses frustration or harsh judgment directed AT the OP without any constructive guidance; (4) It reads as a flame, rant, or personal attack rather than a helpful contribution. Score lower (less likely to violate) when: (1) Criticism or bluntness is paired with actionable legal advice; (2) Negative framing is used to redirect the OP toward a better course of action; (3) The commenter clearly intends to help even if the tone is harsh; (4) The comment is a short joke, quip, or off-topic remark not directed hostilely at the OP; (5) The negative language is directed at a third party (not the OP) and the commenter is expressing empathy or solidarity with the OP; (6) The comment is brief meta-commentary or humor about the thread context rather than an attack.

suggestions.reasoning: All six labeled examples are marked compliant despite containing content that could superficially trigger the current checklist item. Examples include: a vague rhetorical question about OP's situation (dfmlj3k), emotional venting about third parties with no legal advice (dflpz9u), a blunt factual statement about OP breaking the law (dfmhk1f), a humorous generalization about a group (dfls2aw), a meta-comment about a username (dfm4zaa), and a pop-culture joke (dfl5hmj). None of these are actual flames directed hostilely at the OP. The current rubric does not sufficiently distinguish between (a) hostile personal attacks on the OP and (b) off-topic remarks, jokes, emotional asides, or blunt factual statements. The revised rubric explicitly carves out these categories to reduce over-triggering, and the threshold is slightly raised to 0.75 to reflect that the bar for removal should be higher confidence in a genuine flame.

proposed_change.note: This item flags very short comments for closer human or downstream review context; it is not itself a removal trigger. suggestions.reasoning: Four of the six compliant examples (dfmlj3k, dfls2aw, dfm4zaa, dfl5hmj) are very short comments (under 120 characters) that contain no legal advice but also no real flame. The existing checklist item could over-trigger on these since they are 'lacking in advice.' suggestions.description: Refine the prompt template and rubric to better handle short, contextual comments that are clearly part of a real legal thread rather than standalone hypothetical or political posts. The current checklist may over-trigger on brief comments embedded in real legal threads.
suggestions.reasoning: All four labeled examples are compliant short comments (e.g., 'How else do you get the dog?', 'When in doubt, yield, safely.', a question about a custody situation, and a brief quip). These comments are posted within real legal advice threads and clearly relate to real legal situations. The current prompt template and rubric do not explicitly address comments vs. top-level posts, nor do they account for context inherited from a parent thread. A short comment that seems ambiguous in isolation could be incorrectly flagged as hypothetical or off-topic without considering its parent thread context. Adding guidance to consider post type (comment vs. top-level post) and thread context will reduce false positives on compliant short comments.

proposed_change.description: Is the post hypothetical, a general law discussion, or substantially political in nature rather than a real personal legal question?
proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: Hypothetical posts, political posts, law discussions should be posted on /r/legaladviceofftopic. Questions and comments that are substantially political in nature are outside the scope of the sub

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this post is hypothetical, a general law discussion, or substantially political in nature, rather than a genuine personal legal question or comment from someone participating in a real legal situation. If the post is a comment (not a top-level post), consider its role within the thread: short comments responding to or building on a real legal situation described elsewhere in the thread should not be treated as hypothetical or political on their own. Does the post (1) pose a 'what if' or 'hypothetically speaking' scenario rather than describing a real situation? (2) debate legal theory, policy, or how laws work in the abstract? (3) center on political opinions, political figures, or policy debates rather than seeking or providing actionable legal advice? (4) invite debate or opinion on legal or political topics? If the post is a comment embedded in a real legal thread and merely asks a clarifying question or offers brief practical guidance related to the thread's real legal situation, it should not be considered a violation.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The post is a top-level submission that poses a hypothetical scenario ('what if someone did X', 'hypothetically speaking...') rather than describing a real situation the author is experiencing; (2) The post is a general discussion of law, legal theory, or how laws work in the abstract rather than a specific legal problem; (3) The post is substantially about politics, political figures, political events, or policy debates; (4) The post invites debate or opinion on legal or political topics rather than seeking concrete legal guidance; (5) There is no identifiable person with a real legal need anywhere in the post or its thread context. Score lower (less likely to violate) when: (1) The post describes a specific real-life situation the author or someone they know is facing and asks for practical legal advice, even if the underlying issue touches on politically charged topics; (2) The post is a comment within a thread clearly centered on a real legal situation, asking a clarifying question or offering brief practical guidance relevant to that situation — even if the comment itself is short or does not restate the full context.

suggestions.description: Refine the rubric and prompt to better handle edge cases revealed by labeled examples: (1) comments that debate or critique another commenter's viewpoint rather than helping OP (labeled violating), and (2) dismissive/mocking one-liners about the OP or their situation (labeled compliant but arguably mockery). Also clarify that comments engaging in back-and-forth debate with other commenters — even when substantive — are off-topic relative to the sub's helping purpose.
suggestions.reasoning: Three key patterns emerged from the labeled examples: (A) Violating examples d0e47982 and 6137e58d and e05954a6 show that comments addressing other commenters or debating their points — even constructively — are treated as violations rather than genuine help to OP. The current rubric mentions 'fighting with other posters' but doesn't clearly flag substantive debate/critique of other commenters as a violation. (B) Compliant example 62c792b7 ('Oh wow he's so woke cuz he reads the New York Times lmaooo') is a dismissive, mocking one-liner that seems joke-like yet was labeled compliant — suggesting that mocking/sarcasm directed at a third party (not OP) in context of a discussion thread may be tolerated. (C) Compliant examples b2d5998a and 6a9778d2 show that short factual or debating replies to other commenters can be compliant. The rubric threshold and prompt need to more precisely distinguish: comments primarily addressing OP's situation (compliant) vs. comments primarily addressing another commenter's argument (violating when it becomes a tangent), while not over-flagging brief factual replies.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this comment is genuinely helping the person who posted the relationship question. The comment should be assessed for whether it provides advice, support, or constructive perspective to the original poster (OP). Does this comment instead: consist of abuse or personal attacks against OP or another commenter; mock or make jokes at someone's expense with no helpful intent; argue about subreddit rules or moderation (meta-arguments); engage in extended back-and-forth debate or argument with another commenter rather than focusing on OP's situation; go on a tangent that is primarily about disputing another commenter's viewpoint rather than addressing OP's problem; or otherwise fail to contribute meaningfully to helping OP? Note: A brief factual reply or clarification directed at another commenter may still be compliant if it adds relevant information to the discussion. However, a comment that is primarily a rebuttal or critique of another commenter's argument — with little to no relevance to helping OP — should be considered a violation.
logic.rubric: Score high (more likely to violate) when the comment: (1) Is directed at insulting, mocking, or attacking the original poster or another commenter rather than helping OP; (2) Is a joke or sarcastic remark that provides no genuine advice or support to OP; (3) Argues about subreddit rules, moderation decisions, or other meta topics; (4) Is primarily a back-and-forth argument, debate, or rebuttal directed at another commenter rather than engagement with OP's situation — even if the content is substantive, if the primary audience is another commenter rather than OP, it is likely a violation; (5) Goes on a tangent completely unrelated to OP's problem; (6) Critiques or dissects another commenter's reasoning or framing at length without offering any new help or perspective to OP. Score low (less likely to violate) when the comment: offers advice, empathy, perspective, or constructive criticism directly aimed at helping OP navigate their situation; provides a brief factual clarification or relevant information even if framed as a reply to another commenter; offers a direct alternative viewpoint to OP even if it indirectly addresses what another commenter said.

Example 'dfmhpbr' (Mariiriin) uses sweeping language ('all men', generalizations about manipulation) and rhetorical framing — surface features the rubric flags — but it is clearly a direct reply in a conversation thread, critiquing manipulative dating advice rather than pushing an ideological agenda. The rubric's criterion (5) about 'sweeping statements about all men/all women' could cause this to over-trigger. logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this post is primarily pushing an agenda rather than sincerely seeking relationship advice or engaging in normal discussion. Does the post seem designed to promote a particular viewpoint, ideology, gender narrative, political stance, or social agenda, rather than to genuinely seek help with a personal relationship situation or respond to one?
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The post frames the situation in a way that seems designed to validate or spread a predetermined ideological or political viewpoint rather than seek advice; (2) The post uses the relationship scenario as a vehicle to make broader generalizations about a gender, group, or political position, and this generalization is the clear primary purpose of the post rather than incidental; (3) The post's 'question' is rhetorical and the author seems to already have a fixed conclusion they want readers to affirm, with no genuine personal situation underlying it; (4) The post reads more like a standalone manifesto, rant, or opinion piece than a personal request for help or a contextual reply; (5) The post contains language that signals an agenda (e.g., sweeping statements about 'all men', 'all women', ideological buzzwords used as framing devices) AND these are the primary thrust of the post rather than incidental to a personal situation or a direct rebuttal of specific advice. Score lower when: the post, even if emotionally charged, is clearly centered on a specific personal situation and the author is genuinely asking for guidance; the post is a short reactive or sarcastic comment replying to another comment rather than a standalone agenda-driven piece; the post makes broad statements in the context of directly critiquing or rebutting specific advice given in the thread (even if opinionated or generalized); the post's opinionated or political language is incidental to an otherwise personal and advice-seeking narrative.

suggestions.description: Refine the subjective rubric to better distinguish comments about a third party (e.g., the OP's abusive partner) from comments directed at a community member. The current rubric mentions 'a third party being discussed' as a lower-scoring scenario but does not clearly distinguish between describing a third party's character vs. attacking a community member. logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this post or comment uses disrespectful, hostile, or abusive language directed at another person IN THE COMMUNITY (e.g. the original poster, a commenter, or another participant in the thread). Does the tone attack, demean, or insult a community member rather than critique a situation, behavior, or a third party being discussed (such as someone the OP is seeking advice about)?
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The language directly attacks or demeans a community member (OP, commenter, etc.) rather than their actions or situation; (2) The tone is contemptuous, mocking, or hostile toward a community participant; (3) There is name-calling, personal insults, or aggressive commands directed at someone in the thread; (4) The content would make a reasonable community member feel attacked or humiliated. Score lower when: (1) Harsh words appear in a quote; (2) Strong or negative characterizations describe a third party outside the community (e.g., an abusive partner, a boss, an ex) rather than a community member; (3) The language is part of supportive advice that describes the OP's situation or the behavior of someone the OP is discussing; (4) The content is clearly venting about a non-community-member.

suggestions.description: Refine the tone-based subjective check to reduce over-triggering on sarcastic or dismissive remarks about third parties or general situations rather than community members. The compliant example (dfmespi) uses a mocking tone ('lmaooo give me a break') about the OP's partner's reading habits, not about the OP or another commenter.
suggestions.reasoning: The compliant example (dfmespi) is sarcastic and dismissive in tone ('Oh wow he's so woke... give me a break'), but it mocks a third party (the OP's partner), not a community member. The current rubric could over-trigger on this since it mentions sarcasm and contemptuous tone generally. Adding explicit guidance that the target of disrespect must be a community member (OP, commenter, or thread participant) will reduce false positives.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this post or comment is disrespectful in tone toward another person IN THE COMMUNITY — such as the original poster, a commenter, or another thread participant — even without explicit insults or slurs. Does it demean, belittle, dismiss, or condescend toward a community participant? Consider the overall tone and framing, and importantly, identify WHO the disrespect is directed at.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The content is dismissive or mocking of the OP or another commenter specifically; (2) The commenter lectures or shames the OP in a harsh, unkind way; (3) The response is sarcastic or contemptuous toward another community participant rather than being constructive; (4) The post/comment belittles a community member's feelings, intelligence, or worth as a person. Score lower when: (1) The content is blunt but well-intentioned or offers tough-love advice with empathy; (2) The sarcasm, mockery, or dismissiveness is directed at a third party outside the community (e.g., the OP's partner, boss, or ex) rather than a community member; (3) The content critiques a behavior or situation rather than attacking a community member as a person; (4) Strong characterizations describe someone the OP is seeking advice about.

suggestions.description: Refine the creative writing rubric to explicitly score lower for short, casual, low-effort comments that lack narrative structure — preventing over-triggering on genuine (if dismissive or snarky) comments.
suggestions.reasoning: The single labeled compliant example ('Oh wow he's so woke cuz he reads the New York Times lmaooo give me a break') is a short, informal comment with no narrative arc, no polished prose, and no fictional tropes. The existing rubric focuses on 'polished prose' and 'narrative arcs' which this post clearly lacks, so the risk of over-triggering here is low — but adding an explicit note to score lower for brief, casual, low-effort comments strengthens the guardrail and better aligns the checklist with this compliant example.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this post appears to be a creative writing project or fictional scenario rather than a genuine personal relationship situation the author is experiencing. Consider the narrative style, level of dramatic detail, and whether the post reads like a story crafted for entertainment rather than an authentic request for advice. Note: short, casual, or low-effort comments (even if snarky or dismissive) are very unlikely to be creative writing projects.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The post reads like a short story with polished prose, narrative arcs, or fictional tropes (e.g. dramatic twists, overly cinematic descriptions); (2) The scenario is implausibly elaborate or sensationalized beyond what a real person seeking advice would write; (3) The post lacks genuine distress, uncertainty, or a real question — it reads as entertainment rather than a cry for help; (4) The writing style is inconsistent with someone emotionally involved in the situation (e.g. detached omniscient narration, excessive metaphor, cliffhanger endings). Score lower when: the post is messy, emotionally raw, or imperfect in ways consistent with genuine distress; OR the post is a short, casual, one-sentence or informal comment with no narrative structure whatsoever.

suggestions.description: Refine the trolling rubric to clarify that brief snarky or sarcastic comments directed at another person (e.g. mocking someone in a thread) do not automatically qualify as troll posts — troll posts must be bad-faith submissions seeking to provoke the broader community, not just casual dismissive commentary.
suggestions.reasoning: The labeled compliant example ('Oh wow he's so woke cuz he reads the New York Times lmaooo give me a break') is a sarcastic comment that could superficially trigger the 'winking or self-aware humor' criterion (point 2) or 'designed to provoke strong emotional reactions' (point 3) in the current rubric. However, it is a short reply comment directed at a specific person in a thread, not a bad-faith submission designed to bait the community. The rubric needs to distinguish between a dismissive/sarcastic comment and an actual troll post.

logic.prompt_template: Evaluate whether this post appears to be a troll post — content submitted in bad faith to provoke, shock, or entertain rather than to genuinely seek relationship advice. Consider whether the scenario is designed to outrage or amuse rather than to get help. Note: brief sarcastic or dismissive replies directed at another commenter are not troll posts — trolling requires a bad-faith submission targeting the broader community or fabricating a scenario to bait reactions.
logic.rubric: Score higher (more likely to violate) when: (1) The situation described is deliberately absurd, implausible, or designed for shock value; (2) The post includes winking or self-aware humor that signals the author does not take the situation seriously AND the post is a top-level submission or extended narrative rather than a short reply; (3) The post seems designed to provoke strong emotional reactions (outrage, disgust, disbelief) from the community at large rather than seek actionable advice; (4) The 'question' at the end is insincere or rhetorical. Score lower when: the post is earnest, emotionally grounded, and asks a clear genuine question even if the situation seems unusual; OR the post is a short, casual comment or reply directed at a specific person rather than a fabricated scenario targeting the community.

proposed_change.rule_text_anchor: abuse, bigotry, misogyny, misandry, racism, homophobia, gendered slurs, agendas and encouraging violence are not tolerated

logic.rubric: Score HIGH (violation) when: (1) The author uses slurs to demean a person or group; (2) The author genuinely encourages harming someone; (3) The language is directed aggressively at a person or group with clear intent to degrade or threaten. Score LOW (not a violation) when: (1) The term appears in a direct quote from someone else being criticised; (2) The context is clearly educational or analytical; (3) The phrasing is hypothetical without real encouragement; (4) The author names or labels a harmful behaviour (e.g. calling manipulation tactics 'abusive') specifically to condemn or warn against it rather than to perpetrate it; (5) Strong negative language describes a specific individual's conduct in the context of seeking or giving advice, without targeting an identity group.
